FOREWORD

Dear Students,

Welcome to Domasi College of Education (DCE), one of the reputable institutions that train Secondary
School teachers in Malawi. DCE strives to provide its students with a stimulating environment for

title: Handbook
text:
ives to provide its students with a stimulating environment for
academic growth and personal development, quality resources, and an up-to-date curriculum. All these
are designed to help you gain the relevant skills and experience to realize your potential and contribute
to the development of our nation.

We have provided this revised handbook to serve as a reference guide while you pursue your degree
programme here at DCE. The revised handbook will provide you an introduction to the College and

title: Handbook
text:
ed handbook will provide you an introduction to the College and
offer a guide to pertinent policies, processes, and procedures that relate to your academic and social life
as a student. The handbook also provides a listing of support services available to all students at the
College. It is therefore important for you to take time to read and familiarise yourself to this handbook.
Please note that rules and penalties will not be exempted just because someone is unaware or ignorant

title: Handbook
text:
ill not be exempted just because someone is unaware or ignorant
of specific rules and procedures.

This DCE revised student handbook takes cognizance of emerging issues impacting student
participation in higher education in Malawi.

Thank you for choosing Domasi College of Education. I wish you a fruitful and productive time at DCE!

Dr. A. M. Yambeni
Principal

PREAMBLE
1.1 Introduction

Domasi College of Education (DCE) endeavors to adhere to policies of the three thematic areas of the

title: Handbook
text:
eavors to adhere to policies of the three thematic areas of the
Ministry of Education, which are: access and equity; quality and relevance; as well as governance and
management. This necessitates development of various policies in the College to reflect the broad policy
thematic areas highlighted above. The College policies include Registration policy, Assessment Policy,
HIV and AIDS policy, Gender policy, and Transport Policy.

Every registered student who has been admitted to DCE is bound by these regulat

title: Handbook
text:
ions as prescribed and
amended by the College’s Management from time to time.

Order is a prerequisite for the smooth running of any institution or organisation. To ensure that there is
order, specific rules and regulations are necessary. Student’s Rules and Regulations are designed to
ensure that all students have an enabling environment to successfully complete their studies; and that
individual’s fundamental rights and freedoms shall at all times be respected and protected as stipulated

title: Handbook
text:
oms shall at all times be respected and protected as stipulated
in the Constitution of the Republic of Malawi.

With mutual understanding and consideration by all members of the College community, the
framework of rules and regulations can only assist in completing the educational process and moulding
the students into good citizens. Students are expected to adhere to these rules and regulations, and as a
general principle to conduct themselves at all times whether on campus or away from it in a manner

title: Handbook
text:
lves at all times whether on campus or away from it in a manner
that would promote the good name of students themselves and above all, the good reputation of the
College.

Ignorance of these rules and regulations contained in this document is no excuse. No student in
whatsoever capacity, is above these rules and regulations and any other rules and laws of Malawi.

SECTION 4
CHAPLAINCY AND RELIGIOUS WORSHIP

Freedom of worship is a fundamental human right as enshrined in the Constitution of the
Republic of Malawi. As such DCE provides for such an environment in the following ways:

Students have the freedom to worship and participate in activities of any faith group recognised
by the College.

All religious activities in the College shall be coordinated through the office of the College

title: Handbook
text:
College shall be coordinated through the office of the College
Chaplain or in his/her absence, coordination shall be done through the office of the Dean of
Students.

The Chaplain shall also be responsible for providing counselling and spiritual guidance to
students.

The Chaplain shall work closely with the Dean of Students to ensure that the spiritual and social
needs of the students are met.

The Chaplain’s office shall set up a database pertaining to religious affiliation of every student as

title: Handbook
text:
atabase pertaining to religious affiliation of every student as
a member of the College Community to facilitate effective delivery of assistance when need
arises.

Students are encouraged to join students’ religious organizations recognized by the College.
Such organisations shall operate on College Campus upon approval by College Management
through the office of the Dean of Students to enhance students’ religious life.

The Office of the Chaplain is open to all students of different religious backgrounds.

title: Handbook
text:
The College Chaplain will minister to all students regardless of their religious backgrounds.
Faith group gatherings at the College Campus shall ensure that there is order and respect for
others in the College. Students are expected to observe religious tolerance at all times.

In case an organisation expresses interest to use College facilities and services, students should
consult the Office of the Registrar or the Office of the Dean of Students.

title: Handbook
text:
Office of the Registrar or the Office of the Dean of Students.

All religious activities shall take place outside the teaching timetable.

title: Odel
text:
Open Distance Learning
Domasi College of Education is committed to expanding access to quality teacher education through Open, Distance, and e-Learning (ODeL). This flexible mode of learning is designed to cater to aspiring and in-service teachers who may not be able to attend traditional, face-to-face classes due to work or personal commitments
What is ODeL?
ODeL is an innovative approach that integrates online learning, digital resources, and face-to-face interactions to provide an accessible and efficien

title: Odel
text:
t learning experience. Through this mode, students can engage with course materials, interact with instructors, and participate in discussions from anywhere, making education more inclusive and adaptable to diverse learning needs.

Flexible Learning
Students can access learning materials anytime and anywhere, both in print and digital mode.
Blended Approach
Combines online learning, printed materials, and occasional face-to-face interaction.

title: About Dce
text: Domasi College of Education (DCE) was established in response to a shortage of secondary school teachers in Malawi, which was identified in the early 1990s. Initially, Domasi Teachers’ Training College focused on training primary school teachers, but in 1993, it was upgraded to train secondary school teachers. It started by offering a two-year Diploma in Education, later expanded to a three-year program in 1997, including new departments such as Social Studies.

title: About Dce
text:
gram in 1997, including new departments such as Social Studies. Over the years, the College introduced new faculties, courses, and teaching methods, including open and distance learning (ODL) in 2000 with support from CIDA. It expanded further in 2008 by adding Agriculture Education with funding from the African Development Bank. In 2016, the College
transitioned from diploma programs to Bachelor’s Degrees in Secondary Education, establishing four faculties: Natural and Applied Science, Education, Language

title: About Dce
text: Arts and Communication, and Social Sciences. Currently, DCE has 19 academic departments, offers mature entry programs for diploma holders, and has a Centre for Distance and Continuing Education. Enrollment has grown to over 4,000 students. The College is located in Domasi, Zomba, covering 48 hectares, and features modern facilities, including administration blocks, hostels, laboratories, and demonstration schools. DCE operates under the Malawi Government and is affiliated with the University of Malawi.

title: About Dce
text:
awi Government and is affiliated with the University of Malawi. It enjoys some autonomy in academic matters and follows national higher education selection procedures. The College continuously reviews its curriculum to align with local and global education needs and emphasizes research in its programs

Our Mission
To train teachers who shall make positive contribution to national de

velopment.
Our Mandate
To train secondary school teachers and lecturers for teacher training.
Relations with Other Institutions
The College enjoys cordial relationships with other institutions involved in education in Malawi such as the University of Malawi and Malawi Institute of Education. The College works closely with the University of Malawi, whose Senate approves the programmes and curricula, and final awards to the graduating students. has partnered with a number of universities and stakeholders in v

title: Mature Entry 2
text:
Programme Overview
Our mature entry programme provides a unique pathway for experienced educators to advance their qualifications. Tailored for individuals with a minimum of two years teaching experience and the required diploma credentials, this programme blends academic rigor with practical training.
Entry Requirements
-
a) Possess a Diploma in Education (secondary) obtained from Domasi College of Education or any other accredited institution of higher learning.

title: Mature Entry 2
text:
ucation or any other accredited institution of higher learning.
b) Alternatively, possess a diploma in relevant content subject areas plus a University Certificate of Education (UCE) or its equivalent obtained from an accredited institution of higher learning.
c) MUST provide evidence that they successfully completed a twelve (12) week Teaching Practice (TP) session as part of their Diploma in Education or UCE studies.
d) Candidates MUST also meet the additional entry requirements for each programme as outl

title: Mature Entry 2
text:
ined below.
e) Candidates MUST have a minimum of 2 years teaching experience after obtaining their diploma (for teachers serving in public schools only).
f) Candidates MUST not be more than 50 years of age (for teachers serving in public schools only)

025. However, the fee is subject to revision from time to time.

title: Campus Life
text:
STUDENT SUPPORT
DCE realises that coming to College can be stressful. We would therefore like you to join us to learn, relax, and enjoy as you achieve your potential during your studies. You may not need any of the student support services we offer, but it will still be helpful to you to simply know that they exist.
Starting a first-degree programme can be hectic for several reasons. This could be because you are living with complete individual independence for the first time, or you are to cope with a new

title: Campus Life
text:
level of academic rigour. DCE management takes new students through an orientation programme to make the transition from secondary school easier. During the orientation session, you will connect with academic and administrative staff members, register for classes, make friends, learn more about resources available on campus and outside (i.e shopping, accommodation, and banking facilities), and discover ways to get involved in some activities.
RESEARCH AND PUBLICATIONS

title: Campus Life
text:
s to get involved in some activities.
RESEARCH AND PUBLICATIONS
Realising the importance of publishing for an academic institution, the College established a research and publications unit. The unit is managed by members of the academic staff appointed by the Principal. The unit coordinates the publications of both academic and non-academic books, written by DCE staff and students, as well as other people within the community. It also coordinates the publications of research work conducted by the College

title: Campus Life
text:
ates the publications of research work conducted by the College
STUDENT ACCOMODATION
With only 804 bed spaces on campus, students’ accommodation is still very limited. The hostels are divided into male and female categories. The rooms on campus are designed for the occupation of two students, and are furnished with reading tables and chairs, lockers, and beds. You only have to bring your belongings. You will have to apply and be subjected to computer random selection, which removes any bias in the selection

title: Campus Life
text:
process. Males and females are selected separately
You will be required to pay a rental fee per month, per occupant, for the twin room as an undergraduate student. The accommodation fees are revised from time to time by management.
STUDENT HEALTH AND SPRITUALITY
College has well qualified medical personnel. By virtue of being a student at DCE, you are allowed access to medical services at the clin ic at a fee of K10, 000.00 per academic year. Psychosocial counselling: Life at College can be stressful, and

title: Campus Life
text:
students have different abilities of coping with such stress. The College also provides counselling services to students who may experience any form of mental stress, either emanating from academic or social life.
Although DCE is a secular institution, it recognizes individual rights for students to hold their own individual religious engagements. Students are allowed to congregate according to their religious affiliation. Additionally, religious groups are free to book classrooms for their meetings
SPORTS

title: Campus Life
text: Whether you are an active sports person, or simply interested in physical fitness or just wanting to socialise, DCE has plenty of opportunities for your comfort. DCE football ground is a full size-11-a-side pitch located in a breeze of Domasi River to the east of the main campus. It is a multipurpose pitch since different markings can be made to accommodate a variety of sporting activities. Additionally, the tracks around the pitch are regularly used in competitive athletics.

title: Campus Life
text:
s around the pitch are regularly used in competitive athletics. The College has also basketball, netball and volleyball courts. The College has also a gymnasium purposely built to accommodate various games in a covered arena. Such games include, hockey, basketball, netball, volleyball and tennis, among others
SPECIAL NEEDS
DCE has a Special Needs Unit which is housed close to the library building. Facilities in this section help to increase access to learning materials for students with special needs.

title: Campus Life
text:
e access to learning materials for students with special needs. For example, this sec tion has facilities such as computers installed with specialised software including Jaws Dongle for the hearing impaired, braille printing machines and screen magnifiers for students with low vision.
Campus Banking
Easily open and manage your bank account within the university.
Internet Connectivity
DCE is one of the few college/university campuses in the country where students and staff have access to WI-FI.

On Thursday, 29th January 2026, a delegation from the French Embassy in South Africa, Lesotho, and Malawi visited Domasi College of Education (DCE) to explore opportunities to strengthen the teaching of French as a foreign language in Malawi. The delegation met with DCE leadership, including Principal Dr. Arkangel Mtipe Yambeni, Deputy Principal Dr.

title: Alumni
text:
ding Principal Dr. Arkangel Mtipe Yambeni, Deputy Principal Dr. Gilbert Phiri, the Dean of the Faculty of Language, Arts, and Communication, the Academic Registrar, and representatives from the Department of French.
During the discussions, DCE reaffirmed its commitment to expanding French language education by training more teachers and introducing beginner-level courses for students without prior knowledge of the language. The Embassy’s Education and French Cooperation Attaché, Mr. Johann Robinet, and Ms.

title: Alumni
text:
Inès Schmitt, Cooperation Attaché to Malawi, welcomed these initiatives and pledged support in pedagogy and teaching materials. Both parties also emphasized the importance of establishing a long-term collaboration framework.
The delegation endorsed plans to create a “French Corner” and a resource center at DCE to provide authentic exposure to the French language and culture for students, faculty, and the wider community. Additional discussions with the French Department highlighted staff development opportu

title: Alumni
text:
nities through online and in-person training workshops, subject to funding availability, as part of broader efforts to enhance French language education.
In his remarks, Dr. Yambeni outlined his vision for the Faculty of Language, Arts, and Communication, which includes constructing a dedicated facility for the English, French, and Creative and Performing Arts departments. He appealed for continued support from the French Embassy and the international community to realize this vision.

Embassy and the international community to realize this vision.

title: Experiences Of Non Specialist Social Studies Teachers In Teaching
text:
Experiences of non-specialist Social studies teachers in teaching
This is a qualitative study that sought to explore the coping mechanisms of non-specialist Social Studies teachers. The study employed the concept of coping as advanced by Lazarus and Folkman (1984). This framework explains that non-specialist Social Studies teachers use adaptive or maladaptive coping mechanism in order to address their inadequacy in content and pedagogy. This study explored the sampled Social Studies teachers’ experiences, c

title: Experiences Of Non Specialist Social Studies Teachers In Teaching
text: hallenges and their coping mechanisms in teaching. The following main research question guided the study: What are coping mechanisms of non-specialist Social Studies teachers in CDDSs? To explore this question further, the study answered the following specific research questions: (i) what are the pedagogical experiences of non-specialist Social Studies teachers in CDSSs? (ii) What challenges do non-specialist Social Studies teachers experience in their teaching?

title: Experiences Of Non Specialist Social Studies Teachers In Teaching
text: pecialist Social Studies teachers experience in their teaching? (iii) How do non-specialist Social Studies teachers cope with their teaching? Four non-specialist Social Studies teachers were purposively sampled. Data was generated through face-to-face interviews, lesson observations and post-lesson observation interviews. This study found that the sampled non-specialist Social Studies teachers faced challenges in both content and pedagogy. As such, they used adaptive and maladaptive coping mechanisms to dea

title: Experiences Of Non Specialist Social Studies Teachers In Teaching
text:
l with their inadequacy. Adaptive coping was due to their orientation on generic issues in teaching while maladaptive coping was due their knowledge gap on the philosophical foundations, aims and goals for the teaching of Social Studies. Based on these findings, it is necessary to address maladaptive coping mechanisms and enforce adaptive coping mechanisms.
